title: Operational interpretation of the reverse sandwiched Renyi divergences in composite quantum hypothesis testing
abstract: We study the Hoeffding regime of composite quantum hypothesis testing, in which each hypothesis is specified by a sequence of sets of quantum states. We establish quantum Hoeffding bounds under a set of structural assumptions, orthogonal to those of our previous framework. A notable consequence is the direct operational interpretation of the reverse sandwiched Renyi divergence for $α\in (0,1)$: for the task of discriminating a thermal equilibrium state from a probe state subject to unknown dephasing in the energy eigenbasis, with free Hamiltonian evolution as a special case, the optimal Hoeffding exponent is given exactly by this divergence evaluated on a single copy of the system. The same task in the Stein regime is governed by the reverse quantum relative entropy, providing its operational interpretation as well. This behavior contrasts both with the simple independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) setting, where the Petz Renyi divergence and the Umegaki relative entropy govern the Hoeffding and Stein exponents, respectively, and with many composite settings, where only regularized many-copy formulas are available. This finding reveals that passing from simple to composite hypotheses can fundamentally change which quantum divergence determines the operational limits of discrimination, and suggests a new avenue for seeking operational interpretations of quantum divergences by lifting simple hypotheses to richer composite scenarios.
summary: This paper establishes new error bounds for quantum hypothesis testing when dealing with sets of states rather than single states. It provides the first operational meaning for the reverse sandwiched Renyi divergence by showing it determines the error rate when distinguishing thermal states from dephased probe states.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish quantum Hoeffding bounds for composite hypothesis testing and provide an operational interpretation for the reverse sandwiched Renyi divergence and reverse quantum relative entropy.
main_result: The authors prove that in the Hoeffding regime, the optimal Type-I error exponent is determined by the single-copy reverse sandwiched Renyi divergence, while in the Stein regime, the Type-II error exponent is governed by the reverse quantum relative entropy.
method: The study uses large deviation theory, Nussbaum-Szkola distributions, and symmetry reduction techniques like twirling and pinching maps, alongside the Gärtner-Ellis theorem.
model_or_system: The physical model involves discriminating a thermal equilibrium state from a probe state subject to unknown dephasing in the energy eigenbasis, which includes free Hamiltonian evolution as a special case.
key_observables: Type-I error (alpha) and Type-II error (beta) probabilities, and their respective asymptotic error exponents.
important_parameters: Renyi parameter alpha in (0, 1), error rate r, and the inverse temperature beta.
main_assumptions: Assumes permutation symmetry (P-invariance), convexity, compactness, and the existence of a strictly convex and continuous asymptotic cumulant generating function; assumes states are full rank.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares various quantum divergences (Petz, Sandwiched, Reverse Sandwiched, Umegaki, and Reverse Relative Entropy) as functions of alpha for non-commuting qubits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of composite hypothesis testing, develops mathematical bounds using symmetry and pinching techniques, applies these to a specific dephasing model, and concludes with the operational interpretation of the derived divergences.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum thermodynamics, as it provides a fundamental link between information-theoretic divergences and the physical task of distinguishing states under environmental noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Comparison of the Petz, sandwiched, and reverse sandwiched R´enyi divergences as functions of α ∈(0, 1) given in Example 5. The dashed line marks the Umegaki relative entropy D(ρ∥σ), and the dotted line marks the reverse quantum relative entropy DR(ρ∥σ). The shaded region highlights the gap between sandwiched and reverse sandwiched divergences.
